persons.arguments_against: The bio carries only a single forename 'John', so the namesake trap nominally applies: another John Woodall (the corpus notes 2 officials with the surname) could in principle be the Bermuda listing. But the counter-hypothesis dies on the specifics: the staff-list entry is for the 'Governor and Commander-in-Chief' of Bermuda, an office held by exactly one person at a time, and the biography states 'gov. and c.-in-c., Berm., 1955–59' — the exact office, place, and span. The 1958 staff list even prints the rank 'Lieut.-Gen. Sir John Woodall' matching the bio's 'Lieut.-Gen. Sir John'. The honours K.B.E., C.B., M.C. printed in the 1956 staff list match the bio's honours with their pre-1959 dates (K.C.M.G. only awarded 1959, consistent with its absence from the 1956–58 listings). No realistic second John Woodall could be Governor of Bermuda in these exact years. The position_sim FAIL (59 vs bar 60) is a pure abbreviation artefact: 'gov. and c.-in-c.' is verbatim 'Governor and Commander-in-Chief'.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second Lieut.-Gen. Sir John Woodall, or any other John Woodall, serving as Governor of Bermuda c. 1955–1959, or a primary source showing the Bermuda governorship in 1956–1958 was held by someone else.

notes: Unique gubernatorial office + exact place/date span + three shared honours + rank match make this airtight despite the single-initial warning. The deterministic position_sim failure is an abbreviation artefact only.
